retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for mercury exposure is highly variable but is dependent on the level of exposure. Significant exposures can lead to coma and death. Minor symptoms may resolve over time. Neurologic symptoms which can be delayed in the presentation may persist for decades. Fetus and children are highly susceptible and can lead to death, permanent neurologic deficits, or mental retardation.

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Mercury has been used for centuries in multiple capacities, including medicinal and widespread industrial use. Because of this, it is a common chemical exposure and environmental pollutant. There are several forms of mercury that lead to different mercury intoxication syndromes. This activity describes the presentation of patients with mercury poisoning and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of mercury poisoning. Summarize the presentation of a patient with mercury poisoning. Discuss the treatment options for mercury poisoning. Explain the importance of improving care coordination amongst the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients with mercury poisoning. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Mercury has been used for centuries in multiple capacities, including medicinal and widespread industrial use. Because of this, it is a common chemical exposure and environmental pollutant. There are several forms of mercury that lead to different mercury intoxication syndromes. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mercury toxicity can be very difficult to diagnose due to nonspecific signs and symptoms and the variability of the onset of the symptoms.  These can easily mimic several other disease processes affecting multiple systems, including renal, gastrointestinal, nervous, and pulmonary.  This is why it is imperative to obtain a complete, thorough history.  A differential diagnosis includes the following list.

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Mercury toxicity can involve many organs, and initially, the diagnosis can be difficult. Thus, it is best managed by an interprofessional team that includes a toxicologist, poison control, emergency department physician, nurse practitioner, nephrologist, and neurologist. The key is to decontaminate the patient as soon as possible to prevent further absorption of mercury. The outcomes of patients with mercury poisoning depend on the presence of neurological deficits, which in many cases cannot be reversed.

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In the emergency department, the most critical facet of the evaluation is a thorough history, including possible exposures and identifying the symptoms consistent with any of the mercury toxicity syndromes.  Initial testing would include a chest radiograph, urinalysis,  and basic bloodwork such as a complete blood count and a metabolic panel. A 24-hour urine collection for mercury levels should be obtained as well as whole-blood mercury levels in the case of organic mercury exposure as there is very little urinary excretion of these compounds. Positive levels confirm exposure, but the levels do not directly correlate with toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Mercury toxicity can be very difficult to identify with multisystem involvement. It is imperative to have a high index of suspicion and obtain a thorough history while always keeping in mind the complex of symptoms found in the toxidromes. Symptoms of mercury exposure and toxicity can easily be misdiagnosed as normal medical problems such as gastritis, GI bleeding, and respiratory distress. A keen awareness leading to early identification and treatment is critical due to the severe and potentially irreversible damage.

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the United States, a 2013 report documented 1300 single mercury exposures, and only 24 had moderate to major effects. However, internationally, there are much higher rates of exposure, including mercury mining in China, small-scale gold mining, and food contaminated with mercury. Two major toxic incidents occurred in Minamata Bay, Japan, and Iraq. Mercury was dumped into Minamata Bay, and members of the community developed toxicity from eating the fish containing methylmercury. In Iraq, over 6000 people developed toxicity from eating bread baked with grain that was treated with methylmercury-based fungicide.

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mercury exists in organic and inorganic forms. The inorganic form could be further subdivided into elemental mercury (also known as "quicksilver") and mercury salts. Elemental exposures are typically from mercury-containing devices such as thermometers. Mercury salt exposures can be seen with disc battery ingestion or laxative abuse. Organic mercury can also be subdivided into short-chain and long-chain compounds. Organic mercury exposure usually occurs from ingestion of contaminated seafood, paints containing mercury, or ingestion/injections of thimerasol. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Primary absorption of elemental mercury is through inhalation, which causes shortness of breath, cough, fever, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, headache, metallic taste, salivation, and visual disturbance. Severe exposure may lead to respiratory distress and failure. Ingested elemental mercury can be metabolized to inorganic mercury salts and can also develop those symptoms. Acute ingestion of organic salts typically will cause a metallic taste and a graying of the oral mucosa. The most common findings of significant ingestion include abdominal pain, hemorrhagic gastroenteritis, acute tubular necrosis, and shock. [8] Subacute mercury salt ingestion can lead to a wide array of GI, neurologic, and renal symptoms, including loose teeth, salivation, burning sensation in the mouth, tremors, erethism, nephrotic syndrome, proteinuria, neurasthenia, and acrodynia. Organic mercury toxicity is primarily neurologic and is usually permanent. The related toxic symptoms typically occur weeks to months after exposure. Early findings of orofacial paresthesias, headaches, tremors, and fatigue can occur. More severe cases can progress to ataxia, blindness, movement disorders, and dementia. In addition to the severe neurologic findings, patients also develop mild renal, GI, and respiratory distress. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Mercury Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Elemental mercury is primarily absorbed by inhalation. There is minimal absorption through ingestion, and it does cross the blood-brain barrier where it can deposit. Elemental mercury is more volatile as it is heated and is readily absorbed when it is aerosolized by vacuuming. Pulmonary, central nervous system (CNS), and renal toxicity are common with elemental mercury. The gastrointestinal tract primarily absorbs inorganic mercury salts, and secondarily they are absorbed through intact skin. The salts mainly cause renal and gastrointestinal (GI) toxicity. Organic mercury is also primarily absorbed by the GI tract and secondarily through intact skin.  Organic mercury toxicity typically leads to neurologic symptoms which are characteristically delayed. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Heavy Metals -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mercury (Hg): The most common source of mercury is methylmercury, found in fish secondary to pollution. [11] In adults, cases of methylmercury poisoning are characterized by the focal degeneration of neurons in brain regions such as the cerebral cortex and the cerebellum. Depending on the degree of in-utero exposure, methylmercury may result in effects ranging from fetal death to subtle neurodevelopmental delays. Consequently, because pregnant women, women of childbearing age, and young children are particularly at risk, the FDA recommends that they avoid eating shark, swordfish, mackerel, and tilefish. [12] Mercury is also an occupational hazard for dentists in countries where amalgam production (filling material for cavities) is still allowed. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Heavy Metal Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: DMSA is very effective in Hg toxicity. BAL is contraindicated in this instance because it increases the levels of both iHg and CH 3 Hg in the brain. Selenium and vitamin E can aid in the treatment of Hg toxicity. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Antimony Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: In the acute setting, concomitant ingestion and accompanying exposure to other toxic agents must be considered. Antimony toxicity may mimic presentations of other heavy metal poisoning such as arsenic, aluminum, barium, bismuth, cadmium, chromium, cobalt, copper, gold, iron, lead, lithium, manganese, mercury, nickel, phosphorus, selenium, silver, thallium, tin, and zinc. Accompanying exposure to other heavy metals may also be present. Careful history-taking and knowledge of routes of exposure may help differentiate. Serum, urine, and hair levels of other heavy metals may also be obtainable.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: This hierarchical knowledge base connects each causative agent to its specific poisoning type, situates those poisonings within larger accidental or unspecified psychoactive poisoning frameworks, and delineates the spectrum of organic mental disorders and intoxication syndromes that may arise, thereby aiding clinicians in agent identification, poisoning classification, and anticipation of associated neuropsychiatric manifestations for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1627
retrieved_docs.content: The toxic effect of an unspecified substance, especially one that is non‑medical in origin (التأثير السام لمادة غير محددة، خاصة غير الطبية من حيث المصدر) is classified as a subcategory of metal poisoning (التسمم بالمعادن). This relationship indicates that exposures to unidentified non‑medical agents can be understood within the broader framework of metal poisoning, which encompasses various toxic effects caused by metals and guides clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 833
retrieved_docs.content: generic unspecified abnormal‑finding categories, all of which fall within the larger umbrella of injuries and conditions arising from external causes, including toxic exposures such as heavy metals and physical mechanisms such as asphyxiation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 87
retrieved_docs.content: These interlinked relationships illustrate how drug dependence, various specific poisonings, and broader toxicity categories intersect, providing a structured framework for diagnosis, coding, prevention, and management of poisoning‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 239
retrieved_docs.content: poisoning, unspecified mental‑effect poisoning, substance‑related disorders, withdrawal syndromes, and related mental‑health conditions, providing clinicians with a structured approach for diagnosis, classification, and treatment of toxic exposures and substance‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2200
retrieved_docs.content: Neurotoxicity (سمية عصبية) is closely linked with tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة), which represents the characteristic morphological change seen in neurotoxic injury. The presence of tissue damage serves as a key indicator of neurotoxic effects on the nervous system, aiding clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and managing conditions where neurotoxic agents cause structural injury to tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 1091
retrieved_docs.content: as having the parent category التسمم بالمعادن (metal poisoning), situating non‑medical toxic exposures as a subset of metal‑related toxicities. Collectively, these relationships illustrate a structured taxonomy that integrates immune disorders, infectious encephalitis, and a spectrum of poisoning categories—ranging from drug‑ and biologic‑induced toxicities to metal and other non‑medical toxic agents—thereby supporting comprehensive understanding and management of diverse medical hazards.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2256
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder due to and following poisoning is a broad category of medical conditions that arise as a result of toxic exposure, and poisoning by nonmedicinal substances is recognized as a specific type within this category. This relationship indicates that any disorder classified under “Disorder due to and following poisoning” can include cases where the toxic agent is a non‑medicinal substance, helping clinicians to organize and address poisoning‑related health issues more precisely.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 232
retrieved_docs.content: patterns. “التأثير السام لمادة غير محددة، خاصة غير الطبية من حيث المصدر” is a branch of metal poisoning, illustrating that non‑medical toxic substances are captured within the broader poisoning framework. Recognizing these hierarchical and associative links among drug classes, specific agents, intoxication syndromes, dependence states and psychiatric categories is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the development of targeted prevention and treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: are organized within injury classification frameworks, share common external cause codes, and often involve similar pathological outcomes such as tissue damage and neurotoxicity, providing a cohesive framework for assessment and management of traumatic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 942
retrieved_docs.content: Lithium poisoning (التسمم بالليثيوم) is recognized as a specific type of metal poisoning (التسمم بالمعادن), placing it within the broader category of toxic effects caused by metals. The broader category of metal poisoning also encompasses the toxic effect of an unspecified, especially non‑medical, substance (التأثير السام لمادة غير محددة، خاصة غير الطبية من حيث المصدر), which is classified as a subclass of metal poisoning. Clinically, lithium poisoning has been associated with schizophrenia (الفُصام), indicating that exposure to excessive lithium can be linked to the manifestation or exacerbation of this psychiatric disorder. Understanding that lithium poisoning is a form of metal toxicity and its potential connection to schizophrenia helps clinicians evaluate and manage patients presenting with psychiatric symptoms that may have an underlying toxic etiology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1395
retrieved_docs.content: Mercurial diuretic, also referred to as Mercurian diuretic, is classified within the “Types and Miscellaneous Diuretics” category, indicating that it belongs to a broader group of diuretic agents that do not fit into the primary classes such as thiazides, loop diuretics, or potassium‑sparing diuretics. Chemically, the mercurial diuretic is an organometallic compound (organokovové sloučeniny), meaning its active moiety contains a mercury atom bonded to organic ligands. This organometallic nature underlies both its diuretic mechanism and its potential for mercury‑related toxicity, influencing how clinicians assess its safety, dosing, and monitoring requirements in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 292
retrieved_docs.content: agents, immune‑system disorders, arthropod‑borne viral encephalitis, and multiple forms of poisoning—including drug‑induced uric‑acid disturbances, alkaloid toxicities, syrup‑related poisonings, and metal‑related toxic effects—are interconnected. Recognizing these links supports comprehensive clinical assessment and management of patients who present with overlapping allergic, infectious, and toxicological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2266
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning by other medications and drugs is classified as a late effect of poisoning due to a drug, medicinal, or biological substance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: metal‑, and non‑medical substance poisonings, as well as related immune disturbances and adverse medical events, are categorized and linked within clinical coding systems, supporting accurate documentation, risk assessment, and patient‑safety management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2088
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “Injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of aircraft” is linked to the morphological finding of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). In turn, the tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة) is associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity). This relationship indicates that injuries resulting from an aircraft crash, even when the intent is unclear, may involve underlying tissue damage that can give rise to neurotoxic effects, highlighting the importance of evaluating both structural and neurotoxic consequences in the clinical assessment and management of such trauma.
